agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands courier location never be shown due to driver retention losses from perceived surveillance.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver employed or contracted by the company to transport parcels from origin to customer address."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to deliver a specific parcel to a customer."},{"term":"Customer visibility","definition":"The degree to which delivery progress information is exposed to the end customer."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The company's ability to keep couriers working on its platform, directly impacted by working conditions and perceived surveillance."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is communicated — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to approximate zone or ETA-only."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — depends on resolution of the core contradiction. Exact GPS makes this a surveillance flashpoint; abstracted progress would mask it naturally."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal stop)","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — exact GPS exposes deviations and amplifies surveillance concern; abstracted view hides them but may confuse customers."},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer address but delivering to a neighbor first","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — exact coordinates would show courier 'ignoring' the customer; ETA-based approach avoids this perception."},{"scenario":"GPS signal loss or inaccurate coordinates in urban canyons","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — if exact GPS is the contract, signal issues create a broken experience; if ETA-based, GPS gaps are invisible to customers."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk in both directions. Losing driver supply (Operations concern) directly constrains delivery capacity and revenue. Losing customer trust/differentiation (Customer Success concern) impacts acquisition and retention. The wrong call here is expensive either way — but driver supply is typically the harder constraint in two-sided courier marketplaces.","operational":"Driver churn has compounding operational cost: recruitment, onboarding, route familiarity loss, coverage gaps. Exact GPS tracking also increases data infrastructure cost (high-frequency location streaming to customers). An abstracted solution (ETA, zone-based) is operationally cheaper and lower-risk.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability, not necessarily surveillance capability. Industry research (DoorDash, Uber, Amazon) shows ETA countdown and stage-based tracking ('Your courier is 3 stops away') satisfy the actual customer need without exposing raw coordinates. Couriers — also users of the platform — experience exact tracking as adversarial.","risk":"CRITICAL: Exposing exact courier GPS coordinates raises GDPR/privacy-law risk in many jurisdictions. Courier location is personal data; sharing it with third parties (customers) requires lawful basis and may require explicit courier consent. This is a legal and compliance risk that neither team has addressed.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability and transparency. Their actual need (when will my package arrive?) can likely be met without exact GPS."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly impacted by surveillance perception. Driver retention is an existential supply-side risk for the business. Their consent and comfort are non-negotiable from a labor and legal standpoint."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Believes transparency is a differentiator. May be conflating 'exact GPS' with 'delivery visibility' — the latter can be achieved through less invasive means."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention metrics. Has empirical evidence (drivers leaving) that current or proposed tracking is harmful."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED. Exact GPS sharing to customers is a personal data processing activity with significant regulatory implications (GDPR Art. 6, CCPA, local labor surveillance laws)."},{"group":"Product/Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a business strategy decision, not a feature decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the specification contains a direct, irreconcilable contradiction between two stakeholder teams, BOTH presenting their position as non-negotiable. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not been consulted on what is almost certainly a regulated activity (sharing employee/contractor location data with third parties). No acceptance criteria can be defined until the contradiction is resolved and legal guidance is obtained.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business contradiction that cannot be resolved at the engineering level","Driver location data is personal data subject to privacy regulation in most jurisdictions","The customer's underlying need is delivery predictability, which does not strictly require exact GPS","Industry standard is abstracted tracking (ETA, map approximation, stage-based), not raw coordinate sharing"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the business prioritizes customer acquisition differentiation over driver retention","Specific regulatory requirements for this company's operating jurisdictions","Whether couriers are employees or contractors (affects applicable surveillance law)","Whether Customer Success has data proving exact GPS drives acquisition vs. general tracking"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith with real business evidence","No executive decision has already been made that one team is unaware of","The company operates in jurisdictions where personal data sharing is regulated"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS coordinates be shown to customers at all times during delivery. Operations requires courier location NEVER be shown to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from equally-weighted stakeholder teams, both presented as non-negotiable. This cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS. Accepts driver retention risk and requires legal review of data sharing.","B: Side with Operations — show no location data. Sacrifices the transparency differentiator entirely.","C: Negotiate a middle ground — show ABSTRACTED location (ETA, zone-based 'nearby', stage-based progress like '3 stops away') that gives customers predictability without surveilling couriers. This is the industry-standard approach (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon).","D: Make it courier-opt-in — let couriers choose their visibility level, with incentives for higher transparency. Respects driver autonomy and may satisfy both teams partially."],"recommendation":"Option C (abstracted tracking) is the strongest recommendation. It resolves the contradiction by reframing 'transparency' as 'predictability' — which is what customers actually want — while eliminating the surveillance concern. Option D (courier opt-in) is a viable enhancement on top of C. HOWEVER: this is an executive-level business decision, not a domain analysis call. I am flagging the contradiction and recommending, but the business must decide."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal/Compliance has not been consulted. Sharing courier GPS coordinates with customers is processing personal location data of workers and is regulated under GDPR, CCPA, and local labor surveillance laws in most jurisdictions. Before ANY implementation, legal review is mandatory.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Engage Legal/Compliance immediately to determine what is permissible","Delay feature specification until legal guidance is received"],"recommendation":"Block this feature specification until Legal/Compliance provides guidance on what courier location data can legally be shared with customers, under what conditions, and with what consent requirements."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct contradiction between two non-negotiable stakeholder requirements makes it impossible to define correct behavior","business_rule_broken":"Cannot encode a business rule when the business has not agreed on what the rule is"},{"violation":"Legal/Compliance review has not been performed for sharing worker location data with third parties","business_rule_broken":"Personal data processing requires lawful basis — this has not been established"}]}
